name: Orbit
description: Orbit is a configuration-driven framework for evaluating the security of multi-agent LLM systems. It provides 12 scenarios across 5 families (coding, desktop, browser, customer service, cooperative allocation), 9 attack types spanning 4 threat categories (injection, compromised agent, collusion, misuse), and 4 defense categories (security prompting, monitoring, guardian agent, dual-LLM). Built as a plugin on Inspect AI.

distribution.name: BrowserART scenario
distribution.description: Web agent safety tasks: 100 harmful behaviors + 10 benign tasks from BrowserART (Kumar et al., 2024). Action space: click, type, scroll, navigate over accessibility tree.

distribution.name: SWE-Bench scenario
distribution.description: Multi-issue coding agent security: 705 harmful / 1140 benign tasks from SWE-Bench (Jimenez et al., 2024). Action space: bash, file edits, tests in sandboxed repos.

distribution.name: RedCode-Gen scenario
distribution.description: Malware code generation benchmark from RedCode-Gen (Guo et al., 2024). Action space: code generation in sandboxed shell.

distribution.name: BigCodeBench scenario
distribution.description: Code generation benchmark from BigCodeBench (Zhuo et al., 2025). Action space: code generation and execution in sandboxed shell.

distribution.name: CodeIPI scenario
distribution.description: Indirect prompt injection for coding agents from CodeIPI (Sinha, 2026). Action space: code completion with injected prompts.

distribution.name: OSWorld / OS-Harm scenario
distribution.description: Computer-use agent safety: 150 harmful / 369 benign tasks from OSWorld (Xie et al., 2024b) and OS-Harm (Kuntz et al., 2025a). Action space: mouse, keyboard, screenshot over virtual desktop.

distribution.name: tau2-Bench scenario
distribution.description: Customer service with policy-bound tool calls and dual-control loop: 164 harmful tasks from tau2-Bench (Barres et al., 2025).

distribution.name: Cooperative allocation scenarios (DCOP)
distribution.description: Three procedurally generated DCOP environments: JiraTicket, Hospital, MeetSched. Source benchmarks: Colosseum (Nakamura et al., 2026), MeetSched (Mahmud et al., 2025). Action space: propose, accept, reject, submit over shared blackboard.

recordSet.name: Threat type taxonomy
recordSet.description: The four threat types supported by Orbit, each grouping one or more registered attack implementations.

field.name: threat_type
field.description: High-level threat category: injection, compromised_agent, collusion, or misuse.

field.name: attack_type
field.description: Registry key for the attack implementation.

field.name: description
field.description: Brief description of the attack mechanism.

data.description: Adversarial prompts appended to agent conversation.
Adversary content written into shared memory.
Payloads propagating through shared codebase edits.
Attacker controls one or more agents.
Compromised agent inserts backdoors alongside fixes.
Compromised agent breaks other agents' work.
Coalition of agents coordinates against system objectives.
User deliberately requests harmful actions via task prompt.

recordSet.name: Defense category taxonomy
recordSet.description: The four defense categories supported by Orbit.

data.description: Hardening instructions injected into agent system prompts.
Replace agent model with a more secure variant.
Pattern-matching monitor on messages and tool calls.
Myopic LLM monitor evaluating each action in isolation.
History-aware LLM guardian reviewing with full context.
Planning separated from interaction with untrusted data.

recordSet.name: Scenario families
recordSet.description: The five scenario families with task counts (harmful / benign).

rai:dataCollection: Orbit integrates existing benchmarks into a unified multi-agent security evaluation framework. No new data was collected from human subjects. Source datasets retain their original licenses and collection methodologies.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Orbit is designed to improve the security of multi-agent AI systems by systematically evaluating attack and defense mechanisms. The benchmark contains adversarial prompts and harmful task descriptions by design, as these are necessary to evaluate agent safety. These should be used exclusively for security research and evaluation.
rai:dataUseCases: Security evaluation of multi-agent LLM systems. Measuring attack success rates, defense effectiveness, and security-utility tradeoffs across diverse task domains and agent architectures.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: No personal or sensitive information. All task data is synthetic or derived from public code repositories and web benchmarks.
rai:dataBiases: Benchmark coverage is weighted toward coding and web tasks. Cooperative allocation scenarios use procedurally generated environments that may not capture all real-world resource allocation dynamics. LLM judge-based metrics (compromise scores) inherit biases of the judge model.
rai:dataLimitations: Not all attack-defense-scenario combinations have been experimentally evaluated due to compute cost.
Some scenarios (BrowserART, OSWorld) require Docker and specific infrastructure, limiting reproducibility on resource-constrained systems.
AgentHarm data carries a safety-use-only license restriction that propagates to downstream users.
BrowserART upstream data is CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 (non-commercial, no derivatives).
